coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanomaterials (CNMs), including carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene, and carbon dots (CDs), have emerged as promising solutions for pharmaceutical waste remediation in aquatic environments. CNMs outperform traditional adsorbents such as activated carbon owing to their high surface area, strong adsorption capabilities, and enhanced reactivity, making them effective in capturing and degrading a wide range of pharmaceutical pollutants. CNTs exhibit exceptional adsorption capacities owing to their porous structure and high surface area, whereas graphene and its derivatives offer enhanced adsorption and catalytic properties. CDs, owing to their excellent water solubility and tunable surface functionalities, have shown potential for both the adsorption and photocatalytic degradation of pharmaceutical contaminants. The synthesis methods for CNMs, including chemical vapor deposition, hydrothermal synthesis, and laser ablation, can be tailored to produce materials with specific characteristics that enhance their performance in pharmaceutical waste remediation. Numerous studies have demonstrated the efficacy of CNMs in removing various pharmaceutical compounds from aqueous solutions, such as antibiotics, anti-inflammatory drugs, and hormones, with superior performance compared to conventional adsorbents, such as activated carbon. However, challenges such as potential nanotoxicity, environmental persistence, and high production costs need to be addressed for the widespread application of CNMs in water treatment. Future research should focus on developing biodegradable and less toxic CNMs, improving synthesis and functionalization techniques for cost-effectiveness, and enhancing the stability and regeneration of CNM-based systems.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanotubes (CNTs) for pharmaceutical waste remediation.

row.entry: The highest removal efficiency, reaching 98.1%, was achieved under specific conditions: an ACT concentration of 45 mg/L, a pH level of 6.5, an adsorbent quantity of 400 mg/L, and a duration of 23 min for the reaction. Adsorption capacity of 256.4 mg/g. Langmuir isotherm model well fitted (R2 = 0.9961). High pH favoured desorption. BET surface area of 640 m2/g.

The highest adsorption capacity reached 19.9 mg/g. The adsorption process adheres to the pseudo-second-order model. Exothermic and spontaneous adsorption was revealed through thermodynamic analysis. The process aligns with both Langmuir and Freundlich isotherms. The effectiveness of batch adsorption is influenced by factors such as adsorbent quantity, temperature, ion concentration, pH levels, and initial concentration.

Complete degradation of sulfamethoxazole (5 mg/L) was achieved within 2 h using nickel ferrite CNT composite (0.025 g/L) with H2O2 under UV-A light. Incorporation of 25 wt% CNT into spinel nickel ferrite significantly enhanced degradation efficiency and mineralization. Effective charge separation and the generation of hydroxyl radicals contributed to improved photocatalytic activity.

Maximum adsorption capacity of 23.1 mg/g using chitosan-CNT hydrogel beads at pH 7. Adsorption follows nonlinear pseudo-first-order kinetics. Thermodynamic studies suggest the process is endothermic and involves both physical and chemical adsorption.

The ZnO/CNT nanocomposite exhibits a peak adsorption capacity of 666 mg/g under acidic conditions at ambient temperature. The adsorption process adheres to the pseudo-second-order kinetic model. This endothermic and spontaneous reaction aligns with the Freundlich isotherm model.

The magnetic carboxylic multi-walled carbon nanotube-metal-organic frame composite (MCNTs–UiO–66–NH2) demonstrated an adsorption capacity of 139 mg/g at 313 K. The adsorption process aligns with both Langmuir and Pseudo-second-order models, exhibiting spontaneous and endothermic characteristics. This composite shows efficacy across a broad pH spectrum and exhibits high regeneration efficiency when treated with NaHCO3.

Composite membranes made of GO and single-walled carbon nanotubes (GO-SWCNT BPs) demonstrated an adsorption capacity of 116 ± 2 mg/g at pH 4. The optimal performance was achieved with a GO content of 75 wt%. These membranes proved to be recyclable and efficient in eliminating NSAIDs, with their effectiveness directly related to the amount of GO present.

Adsorption by green functionalized CNTs, with adsorption capacity ranging from 0.058 to 0.235 mmol/g. Chemisorption was indicated by pseudo-second order model. Equilibrium time was longer for LOS (teq = 20 min) compared to Diclofenac. Surface area and mesoporosity increased after functionalization, improving adsorption efficiency.

The multilayer porous biochar exhibited an Oxytetracycline adsorption capacity of 72.59 mg g−1. The removal process involved various mechanisms, including chemisorption, interactions across multiple layers, and disorganized diffusion. The primary interactions were electrostatic forces, ligand exchange, π-π bonding, hydrogen bonding, and complexation. The biochar's specific surface area was measured at 237.51 m2/g.

Vertically-aligned carbon nanotube (VaCNT) membranes demonstrated negligible adsorption of steroid hormones at a drag force above 2.2 × 10−3 pN. Reducing the drag force to 4.3 × 10−4 pN increased hormone adsorption to 0.4 ng cm−2. At 1.6 × 10−3 pN, hormone adsorption correlated with adhesive van der Waals forces.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Graphene nanomaterials for pharmaceutical waste remediation.

row.entry: GO doped polymer inclusion membranes achieved an optimal extraction efficiency of approximately 84% for Ibuprofen at pH 10 with 0.75% GO concentration. The membranes exhibited better chemical stability in acidic conditions than in alkaline solutions. Further research is needed to enhance their stability and efficiency for aquatic remediation.

The GO/Ca‐Alg2–PAM beads exhibited a peak adsorption capacity of 6.846 mg/g for Ciprofloxacin. The Langmuir isotherm model provided the best fit for the adsorption data, suggesting a uniform adsorption surface. Experiments conducted at various temperatures confirmed that the adsorption process occurred spontaneously.

Nanocomposites GO demonstrated a photocatalytic rate constant reaching 0.0136 min−1 in the degradation of carbamazepine, resulting in more than 90% elimination within 2 h. GO enhanced the photocatalytic performance by increasing surface area, enhancing light absorption, and reducing the band gap. The composites maintained significant reusability and durability across five consecutive cycles. O2 •− and OH• were identified as the key radicals in the photocatalytic degradation process.

The ZnO/GO heterostructured composite catalyst demonstrated >89% removal efficiency for individual steroid estrogens, reaching up to 98%. Real wastewater treatment showed 63–78% efficiency. After three reuse cycles, the catalyst maintained over 80% efficiency. The addition of 1% H2O2 led to 100% photodegradation in 10 min. The primary reactive species responsible for degradation were hydroxyl radicals (HO•).

GO nanosheets exhibited exceptional performance in removing clofibric acid from water, with a peak adsorption capacity of 994 mg/g. This method proved more effective than conventional wastewater treatment techniques. The adsorption process reached equilibrium rapidly, occurring within minutes, and aligned closely with the pseudo-second-order kinetic model.Adsorption was strongly influenced by pH, showing reduced efficiency at pH > 4 and enhanced removal at pH < 4, especially in the presence of humic acid.

The ethylenediamine-crosslinked GO membrane exhibited superior stability and removal performance for penicillin compared to non-crosslinked GO membranes. Penicillin, being negatively charged, showed the highest removal efficiency due to steric effects and electrostatic repulsion. Low concentrations (500 μg L−1) of antibiotics were removed more efficiently than high concentrations (10 mg/L).

The cellulose nanofibril/GO hybrid aerogel demonstrated a peak adsorption efficacy of 418.7 mg g−1 for chloramphenicol, with a removal percentage exceeding 69%. The primary adsorption mechanisms included electrostatic attraction, p-π interactions, π-π interactions, and hydrogen bonding. The aerogel maintained its adsorption performance after ten regeneration cycles, indicating its potential for repeated use in wastewater treatment.

GO exhibited peak adsorption efficacy of 204.08 mg/g for trimethoprim and 13.89 mg/g for isoniazid, with equilibrium reached in less than 1 h. TMP adsorption was favoured in alkaline conditions, while INH adsorption was more effective in acidic environments.

The nanohybrid materials composed of GO and oxidized carbon nanotubes demonstrated exceptional performance in eliminating ranitidine from water-based solutions. These materials exhibited a remarkable removal efficiency of 98.3% and were capable of absorbing 97.8 mg of the drug per gram of material. Optimal conditions included a contact time of 140 min, 10 mg nanohybrid mass, a solution temperature of 290 K, pH of 6.4, 0.5% crosslinking agent, and a GO-to-OCNT ratio of 1:4. The adsorption data fit well with the Langmuir isotherm model, and the process was characterized by pseudo-second order kinetics, confirming exothermic physisorption.

The GO nanoplatelets demonstrated a removal efficiency of 92.68% for prednisolone. Optimal conditions for maximum adsorption included an initial concentration of 10 mg/L, adsorbent dosage of 2.00 g/L, pH of 6, and agitation speed of 120 rpm at 313 K after 60 min. The adsorption process was best described by the Temkin isotherm model and followed pseudo-second-order kinetics.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots for pharmaceutical waste remediation.

row.entry: The Silica CQDs/(TiO2) composite, with an optimal Si-CQDs concentration of 1 wt%, achieved complete degradation of 5 mg/L of acetaminophen within 4 h, demonstrating a 33.3% faster degradation rate than pure TiO2. The incorporation of Si-CQDs enhanced TiO2's photocatalytic ability for effective pharmaceutical waste removal in aquatic environments.

The 5CQDs/TiO2 photocatalyst achieved an 82% removal efficiency for caffeine after 9 h of visible light irradiation. Additionally, it removed 88% of carbamazepine and 84% of ibuprofen, with residual concentrations significantly below reported toxicity levels. The photocatalyst demonstrated good flexibility and maintained activity over five runs without significant loss.

The photocatalyst composite consisting of N–Bi co-doped CDs derived from rice husks and TiO2 exhibited remarkable efficacy. It successfully degraded 5 ppm of DCF completely in 60 min under simulated sunlight conditions and in 180 min when exposed to natural sunlight. The total organic carbon (TOC) removal efficiency was up to 87.63%.

The use of titanium dioxide (TiO2)/CQDs (4% w/w) significantly enhanced the photodegradation of SDZ in both simulated freshwater and brackish water, requiring less than 4 kJ L−1 of UV energy for effective removal. The photocatalytic treatment efficiently eliminated SDZ's antibacterial activity, indicating its potential for treating aquaculture effluents.

The magnetic CNTs, enhanced with CDs and magnetite, showed a remarkable ability to absorb carbamazepine, reaching 65 mg per gram of adsorbent at a pH of 7.0 ± 0.2. The adsorption process was swift, with 80% of the contaminant removed in just 3 h. These CMNTs maintained their effectiveness across a broad pH spectrum and demonstrated impressive durability, losing only 2.2% of their adsorption capacity after six cycles of regeneration.

The synthesized CQDs/TiO2 composite demonstrated significant photocatalytic activity for the removal of SMX from aquaculture effluents. The selected photocatalyst resulted in a remarkable reduction of SMX half-life times (t1/2) by 19–68 times in both phosphate buffer solution (PBS) and synthetic sea salts (SSS), indicating effective degradation.

The synthesized CDs embedded in the metal-organic framework MIL-88 B(Fe) demonstrated excellent photocatalytic activity, achieving up to 90% removal of naproxen from aqueous solutions under visible light, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) value of around 5%. The optimal degradation conditions were pH 4, photocatalyst concentration of 0.2 g L−1, and H2O2 concentration of 40–50 mM. The peak adsorption efficacy was 121.5 mg/g.
The optimized bismuth oxide/CDs composite demonstrated effective removal of amoxicillin, achieving a degradation efficiency of 54.4%.

The CD/ZnO composite derived from coconut achieved a degradation efficiency of 96% for ofloxacin under solar irradiation acting as a photocatalyst. The synthesis involved a hydrothermal method to produce CDs from coconut and ZnO, followed by calcination, which enhanced photocatalytic performance. The removal followed a first-order reaction kinetics, with a rate constant of 0.01 min−1, demonstrating effective degradation of the antibiotic in wastewater.

The carbon quantum dot composite photocatalyst achieved a degradation efficiency of 94.8% for levofloxacin under 60 min of light irradiation. The incorporation of CQDs enhanced light absorption, charge separation, and electron transfer, leading to improved photocatalytic performance compared to the unmodified BOI and CN. Active species such as •OH and •O2− were identified as the primary agents for the degradation process, with possible pathways including defluorination and oxidation of piperazine moieties.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparative Analysis of Carbon Nanomaterials vs. Conventional Adsorbents [11,14].

row.entry: Higher surface area (up to 2630 m2/g for graphene); functionalizable; adsorbs up to 400 mg/g of ibuprofen
Moderate surface area (∼1000–1500 m2/g); adsorbs ∼200 mg/g of ibuprofen
Photocatalytic degradation possible (e.g., CDs); up to 90–95% removal of antibiotics under optimized conditions
Purely adsorptive, no degradation capabilities; achieves ∼70–80% antibiotic removal under similar conditions
High; can be regenerated photochemically or electrochemically; retains ∼90% efficiency after five cycles
Limited; often requires chemical regeneration; efficiency may drop by 40–50% after multiple cycles
High; tunable through surface modifications; GO achieves >90% selectivity for NSAIDs
Low; non-selective adsorption; activated carbon shows ∼60% selectivity for NSAIDs
Higher initial cost (∼$500/kg) but potentially lower in long-term applications
Lower upfront cost (∼$20–100/kg), higher replacement frequency
Low toxicity; green synthesis methods available for some CNMs
Effective in adsorbing antibiotics, NSAIDs, and hormone pollutants
Used in general water purification applications
Emerging technologies are addressing scalability concerns
CNTs remove up to 95% of antibiotics under optimized conditions; GO achieves ∼85% ibuprofen removal
Activated carbon removes ∼70–80% of antibiotics; ibuprofen removal ∼60–70%

row.entry: Adsorption (π–π interactions, van der Waals forces), catalysis (redox reactions)
Adsorption (van der Waals forces, electrostatic interactions), photocatalysis (ROS generation), catalysis
Adsorption (electrostatic and hydrogen bonding), Fenton-like catalysis, photocatalysis
